moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, claiming central space and preparing the Queen's Gambit. The d4 pawn controls c5 and e5, and it's already defended by the queen.
plans: Play c4 next to challenge Black's center
Build a broad pawn center
alternatives: e4 -- leads to more open, tactical games

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black meets the center head-on. By playing ...d5, Black ensures equal central space and prevents White from dominating with e4 immediately.
plans: Hold the center, develop pieces naturally
alternatives: Nf6 -- the Indian approach, more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Queen's Gambit. White offers the c-pawn to pull Black's d5 pawn out of the center. If Black takes, White gets a big center with d4 and eventually e4.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Central tension: White d4+c4 vs Black d5
plans: If ...dxc4, recapture the pawn or play e4 with a huge center
If ...e6, maintain pressure on d5

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the gambit! This is a perfectly valid choice -- Black gives up the center but gains a tempo because White has to spend time winning the pawn back.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black surrenders the center temporarily; White has a d4 pawn vs nothing
plans: Develop quickly, play ...c5 to challenge d4
Don't try to hold the c4 pawn -- focus on activity
alternatives: e6 -- the QGD, solid but the bishop gets locked in

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops naturally and doesn't rush to recapture the pawn. The knight on f3 supports d4, controls e5, and prepares kingside castling.
plans: Develop with e3 + Bxc4 to recover the pawn naturally
Keep flexible development
alternatives: e3 -- also good, immediately opening the bishop's diagonal

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to its best square, controlling d5 and e4. This prevents White from easily playing e4, which would give White a dominant center.
plans: Control e4, prepare ...e6 and ...c5
alternatives: a6 -- preparing ...b5 to hold the pawn, but this is considered slightly dubio...

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens the diagonal for the bishop to recapture on c4. This is the most common and solid approach.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's d4-e3 structure is solid; bishop will recapture on c4
plans: Bxc4 next, then O-O with easy development
alternatives: e4 -- the central advance, more ambitious but also more committal

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black supports the d5 square prophylactically (even though there's no pawn there now) and opens the bishop's diagonal. The e6 pawn prepares ...c5, which is Black's key central break in the QGA.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's e6 pawn supports a future ...c5 break
plans: Play ...c5 to challenge d4
Develop the dark-squared bishop and castle
alternatives: Bg4 -- pinning the knight, active but the bishop can be chased with h3

moveAnnotations.annotation: White recovers the pawn with the bishop landing on a fantastic diagonal. The bishop on c4 eyes f7, the weakest point near Black's king, and controls the a2-g8 diagonal.
plans: Castle next, complete development with a great position
The Bc4 pressures f7 and supports central play
alternatives: No real alternative -- this is the whole point of e3

moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the key break! Black challenges White's d4 pawn immediately. If White takes ...cxd4, the position opens up and Black gets free piece play.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Central tension: Black's c5 challenges White's d4 -- the position is about to open up
plans: Challenge d4, get active piece play in the center
Develop quickly and castle
alternatives: a6 -- preparing ...b5, but ...c5 is more direct and principled

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles, getting the king safe before the center opens up. With ...c5 on the board creating central tension, it's crucial to castle now.
plans: Develop queenside pieces, fight for the d-file
Consider dxc5 or maintain the tension
alternatives: dxc5 -- releasing tension immediately, but castling first is more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays a prophylactic move preventing Nb5, which would target c7 and d6. It also prepares ...b5 to expand on the queenside and kick the Bc4.
plans: Play ...b5 to attack Bc4 and expand on the queenside
Develop ...Bb7 with a strong diagonal
alternatives: Nc6 -- developing a piece, but a6 prepares the whole queenside plan

moveAnnotations.annotation: White places the queen on e2 to connect the rooks and support a future e4 push. The queen on e2 is very flexible -- it supports e3-e4, protects the Bc4 if Black plays ...b5, and stays out of the way.
plans: Prepare e4 central advance
Support the bishop on c4 against ...b5
alternatives: a4 -- preventing ...b5 directly, but Qe2 is more ambitious

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black goes for the queenside expansion as planned. The pawn on b5 attacks the bishop on c4, forcing it to retreat, and claims space on the queenside.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black has a queenside pawn majority that can create a passed pawn later
plans: Develop Bb7 on the long diagonal
Use the queenside majority as a long-term asset
alternatives: Nc6 -- developing, but ...b5 is the thematic move that defines Black's counte...

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop retreats to b3 where it's still on the same powerful diagonal aiming at e6 and f7. On b3, the bishop is out of danger from the b-pawn advance and remains a strong attacking piece.
plans: Maintain diagonal pressure on e6-f7
Prepare Nc3 and e4
alternatives: Bd3 -- points at the kingside but blocks the d-file and doesn't pressure f7

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black fianchettoes the bishop on the long diagonal, which is the payoff for the whole ...a6-...b5 plan.
plans: Pressure the long diagonal, especially e4 and d5
Support central play from the flank
alternatives: Nbd7 -- developing, but the bishop on b7 is the whole reason Black played ......

moveAnnotations.annotation: White places the rook on the d-file, the most important file in this position. If the center opens with dxc5 or cxd4, the d-file will be crucial.
plans: Control the d-file
Prepare Nc3 and e4 break
alternatives: Nc3 -- developing the knight, also very natural

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to d7, supporting e5 and c5 while keeping the c-file clear for the rooks.
plans: Reroute Nd7 to b6 or e5 depending on the position
Castle and complete development
alternatives: Nc6 -- more active but blocks the c-pawn and can be hit by d5

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the last minor piece, adding another attacker to the e4 square. The knight on c3 prepares the thematic e4 advance, which would give White a powerful central pawn duo.
plans: Play e4 for a dominant center
The knight also eyes d5 and b5
alternatives: e4 -- playing it immediately is possible but Nc3 first adds more support

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black places the queen on b6, adding pressure to d4 and b3. This is a multipurpose move -- the queen eyes the d4 pawn, pressures the Bb3, and supports ...c4 if Black wants to close the queenside.
plans: Pressure d4 and Bb3
Support queenside play with ...c4 or ...Rc8
alternatives: Be7 -- simple development, but Qb6 is more active and thematic

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim in the center and controlling the important e5 and c5 squares.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a central pawn on d4, creating the potential for a classical pawn center with c4.
plans: Prepare c4 to challenge Black's center
Develop pieces toward the center with Nf3 and Bf4 or Bg5
alternatives: 1.e4 leads to more tactical, open games

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds symmetrically, placing a pawn in the center and fighting for equal space. this stabilizing move controls key central squares and prepares natural piece development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The position now features symmetrical central pawns, with both sides controlling key central squar.
plans: Prepare to deal with White's coming c4 advance
Develop pieces naturally with Nf6 and Bf5 or Bg4
alternatives: 1...Nf6 transposes to various Indian defenses

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Queen's Gambit! White offers the c4 pawn to deflect Black's d5 pawn and gain central control.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates the Queen's Gambit pawn structure, offering to trade the c4 pawn for Black's central.
plans: Regain the pawn with natural development
Build pressure on the center with pieces
alternatives: 2.Nf3 leads to quieter development without immediate central tension

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the Queen's Gambit, capturing the c4 pawn and temporarily gaining material.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black has captured on c4, removing the central tension but giving White a free hand in the center.
plans: Hold onto the extra pawn with ...b5
Develop quickly to avoid being overwhelmed in the center
alternatives: 2...e6 declines the gambit, leading to slower but more solid positions

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the king's knight to its most active square, controlling the important e5 and g5 squares while preparing to support the center.
plans: Support e4 advance to dominate the center
Develop the kingside pieces toward castling
alternatives: 3.e3 immediately prepares to recapture on c4 but is less forcing

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the king's knight to its ideal square, controlling the central e4 and d5 squares while preparing to castle kingside.
plans: Support ...e6 to develop the light-squared bishop
Prepare castling kingside for king safety
alternatives: 3...e6 immediately supports the center but blocks the light-squared bishop

moveAnnotations.annotation: White prepares to recapture the c4 pawn with the light-squared bishop, maintaining central control while developing pieces harmoniously.
plans: Develop Bxc4 to regain the pawn
Complete kingside development and castle
alternatives: 4.e4 immediately grabs the center but allows Black active counterplay

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strengthens the center and prepares to develop the light-squared bishop, typically to b7 via the long diagonal.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid pawn structure with pawns on e6 and f7, supporting central control while pre.
plans: Develop the light-squared bishop to b7
Prepare ...c5 to challenge White's center
alternatives: 4...Bg4 pins the knight but allows White strong central play

moveAnnotations.annotation: White regains the gambit pawn and develops the light-squared bishop to its most active square.
plans: Castle kingside to complete development
Apply pressure on f7 and the long diagonal
alternatives: 5.Be2 is safer but less active

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at White's central pawn, creating immediate counterplay and fighting for central equality.
plans: Develop pieces actively with tempo
Create counterplay against White's center
alternatives: 5...Be7 is quieter but allows White to consolidate

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles kingside, bringing the king to safety while connecting the rooks. this move also prepares to bring the rook to d1, putting pressure on Black's central pawns.
plans: Bring the rook to d1 to pressure the d-file
Coordinate pieces for central pressure
alternatives: 6.dxc5 releases central tension but gives Black active pieces

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black prepares the thematic queenside expansion with ...b5, controlling the b5 square and preventing White pieces from landing there.
plans: Advance ...b5 to gain queenside space
Develop the bishop to b7 via the long diagonal
alternatives: 6...Nbd7 develops immediately but doesn't prepare queenside expansion

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the queen to a central square, preparing to support the center with Rd1 and maintaining flexibility.
plans: Play Rd1 to dominate the d-file
Maintain central pressure while keeping tactical options
alternatives: 7.Re1 prepares e4 but is less flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black executes the thematic queenside expansion, attacking White's bishop and gaining valuable space. This move forces the bishop to retreat while establishing Black's presence on the queenside.
plans: Develop Bb7 to complete the fianchetto
Use queenside space advantage for piece activity
alternatives: 7...Be7 develops but allows White to consolidate the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: White retreats the bishop to the b3 square, maintaining its activity on the long diagonal while avoiding the pawn attack.
plans: Support central play from the long diagonal
Coordinate with other pieces for kingside pressure
alternatives: 8.Bd3 eyes h7 but is less active on the long diagonal

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto development, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it exerts maximum pressure.
plans: Use the long diagonal to create kingside threats
Support central squares with the powerful bishop
alternatives: 8...Be7 develops but is much more passive

moveAnnotations.annotation: White brings the rook to the central d-file, putting immediate pressure on Black's position and supporting the d4 pawn.
plans: Maintain pressure on the d-file
Support the d4 pawn and central control
alternatives: 9.a4 challenges Black's queenside immediately

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the queen's knight to its ideal square, supporting the center and preparing to contest White's pressure.
plans: Bring the knight to more active squares like b6 or c5
Complete development and challenge White's center
alternatives: 9...Nc6 is more aggressive but blocks the c-pawn

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the queen's knight to its most active square, supporting the d4 pawn and increasing control over central squares.
plans: Support d4 and maintain central control
Coordinate pieces for potential tactics
alternatives: 10.a4 immediately challenges Black's queenside structure

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black activates the queen with tempo, attacking the b2 pawn and creating immediate tactical threats.
plans: Create tactical threats against White's pawns
Coordinate queen and bishop for maximum pressure
alternatives: 10...Be7 completes development but is less active

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Smyslov Variation (3...a6) is an important variation of the Queen's Gambit Accepted. Smyslov's idea of 3...a6 immediately prepares ...b5 to hold the c4 pawn or at least gain queenside space.
Black responds with d5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Smyslov Variation (3...a6).
c4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
